title: Making a simple leather wallet
subtitle: Cut, skive, punch, and hand-stitch a flat bifold wallet in vegetable-tanned leather
excerpt: How to make a flat bifold wallet: cutting the panels from vegetable-tanned leather, bevelling and burnishing the edges, marking and punching the stitch line, and hand-stitching with a waxed linen thread saddle stitch.
sourceNotes: Al Stohlman: The Art of Hand Sewing Leather. Traditional craft sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: The fundamental hand-stitching technique for leather: two needles threaded on a single waxed thread, one at each end. Each needle passes through the same hole from opposite sides, locking the thread in the hole. A saddle stitch is stronger than a machine stitch because each hole is independent; cutting one stitch does not unravel the seam.

Thinning the flesh side of a leather edge with a sharp knife to reduce bulk at folded or overlapping joints. A wallet fold is skived so the folded edge does not stand proud of the leather face; unskived leather folds leave a visible ridge.

content.text: A flat bifold wallet requires vegetable-tanned leather 2 to 3 mm thick for the spine panel, and 1 to 1.5 mm for the card pockets. Thicker leather produces a wallet that does not fold flat.
Skiving
the spine at the fold line brings it to 1 mm and allows a clean fold without cracking.
Materials
Vegetable-tanned leather: one piece 210 × 100 mm (8 1/4 × 4 in), 2 to 3 mm thick (outer panel), and one piece 190 × 95 mm (7 1/2 × 3 3/4 in), 1 to 1.5 mm thick (card pockets).
Waxed linen thread, 0.8 to 1 mm, in a colour to match the leather.
Two blunt harness needles.
A stitching chisel (pricking iron) in 4 mm pitch, for marking and punching the stitch holes.
Step 1, cut the panels

Mark the outer panel and the pocket strip on the grain side of the leather with a pencil against a metal straight-edge. Cut with the leatherwork knife in a single firm stroke per line; multiple passes tear the fibres. Cut away from the body. Check each panel against the template dimensions before cutting; leather cannot be uncut.
Step 2, bevel and burnish the edges
Run a Size 1 or Size 2
edge beveller

along all cut edges on both the grain and flesh sides to remove the sharp corner. Moisten the bevelled edge with a damp cloth, then rub firmly with a wooden leather burnisher in back-and-forth strokes until the edge compresses to a polished, rounded profile. Edges that are not burnished before stitching are difficult to access with the burnisher once the panels are sewn together.
Step 3, mark and punch the stitch line

Mark a stitch line 3 mm (1/8 in) from the edge using a wing divider or stitch groover. Place the pocket strip on the outer panel in its final position. Press the pricking iron firmly into the leather at one corner of the stitch line and strike it with a mallet to punch the first set of holes. Walk the pricking iron along the line, placing the last tooth of the iron in the last hole punched to maintain equal spacing. Punch through both layers together on the side seams.
Step 4, saddle stitch

Cut a length of waxed linen thread approximately 3 times the seam length. Thread a needle on each end. Pass needle 1 through the first hole from front to back. Pull the thread through so both ends are equal. Pass needle 2 through the same hole from back to front. Pull both needles through the second hole: needle 1 from front to back, then needle 2 from back to front, making sure needle 2 passes behind the thread from needle 1. Tighten each stitch evenly. The
saddle stitch
locks at every hole; maintain consistent tension throughout. At the end, backstitch through the last 2 holes and trim the thread ends close.
Common mistakes
Not burnishing edges before stitching.
Once sewn, the seam edges cannot be burnished without dragging the burnisher across the thread. Always finish all edges before assembly.
Pulling one needle through before the other at each hole.
Both needles must be through the hole before either is tightened. Tightening one side first locks the other needle and damages the thread at the hole edge.
